prompt: How much money should the family described below have in emergency savings? 30-year-old white male, employed full time, married, 30-year-old spouse is unemployed. Lives in St. Louis, Missouri, in owned home (no mortgage). Two children ages two and four.
transcript:
I’m Arthur. Without the family’s actual essential monthly expenses, a precise dollar target isn’t possible. For this household, I’d keep **6–9 months of essential expenses** because it relies on one income and supports two young children; the mortgage-free home reduces the target somewhat, but property taxes, insurance, maintenance, healthcare, food, utilities, transportation, and childcare still matter.

**Target formula**

| Essential monthly spending | 6-month fund | 9-month fund |
|---:|---:|---:|
| $3,000 | $18,000 | $27,000 |
| $4,000 | $24,000 | $36,000 |
| $5,000 | $30,000 | $45,000 |
| $6,000 | $36,000 | $54,000 |

**Recommendation:** If their essentials are around **$4,000 per month**, target **$30,000**—7.5 months—as a practical midpoint. Favor the upper end if the employed spouse’s job is unstable, health coverage is expensive after job loss, or the home/vehicles may require major repairs.

Calculate the target using:

> Housing costs excluding mortgage + utilities + groceries + insurance + healthcare + transportation + childcare + minimum debt payments × 6–9

Keep this reserve liquid in an FDIC- or NCUA-insured high-yield savings or money-market deposit account—not invested in stocks. Race and age do not materially determine the target; household dependence on one income, essential spending, job stability, insurance deductibles, and home-repair exposure do.
